ReleaseLabel: docs-only: docs/tutorials/module-team-hello-world-copilot-ado.md (Set 103 'Copilot + Azure DevOps Hello-World Tutorial', Session 1 of 2 authored the draft; no VSIX rebuild, no publish, no version bump)
Notes: AUTHORED IN SESSION 1, NOT YET WALKED. This checklist IS the acceptance test for a docs-only set: the operator quality bar forbids shipping untested step-by-step instructions, and the Azure DevOps + Copilot-seat steps are only runnable with operator-supplied resources. Session 2 runs this walk with the operator against a BRAND-NEW scratch Azure DevOps organization/project (never a real one), a staff-like Copilot seat, and ~half a day; every item's Passes/Result/Feedback is filled in DURING that walk, and the draft banner is removed only after it passes. This walk ALSO discharges Set 102's still-armed Azure DevOps UAT (the live az PR create + finalize round-trip against a real ADO host): Walks 5 and 8 are the exact 102-armed actions, cross-referenced there. WALK ORDER MIRRORS THE TUTORIAL'S NATURAL ORDER (Part 0.6 -> Part 1 -> Part 2 -> Part 3 -> Parts 4-5 -> Part 6 -> Part 7 -> Part 8 -> Part 9 -> Part 10 -> the Part 0.5 no-CLI floor last as an isolated spot check): Walk 1 = Copilot seat CLI setup (Part 0.6); Walk 2 = ADO project/repo/membership + init trunk (Part 1); Walk 3 = Build scaffold with the Copilot seat + auth-preflight OK (Part 2 + Part 0.6's deferred preflight); Walk 4 = starter lifecycle + real modules + branch policies (Part 3); Walk 5 = author ALL THREE session sets (Part 4 greeter + Part 5 clock & integration) with the greeter PR as the live-ADO 102-armed Open PR; Walk 6 = run the greeter AND clock module sessions through Copilot in worktrees (Part 6; integration stays blocked); Walk 7 = author azure-pipelines.yml, PUSH IT FIRST, then register the pipeline + auto-included reviewers + Build validation, the FIRST-EVER live run of the pipeline (Part 7); Walk 8 = complete the greeter AND clock PRs on ADO + Finalize both (Part 8; the greeter round-trip is 102-armed); Walk 9 = the now-unblocked integration set auto-requests both owners (Part 9); Walk 10 = the FULL tag + hotfix (PR/CI/validate/deploy/complete/cleanup) + rollback drills (Part 10); Walk 11 (OPTIONAL) = the no-CLI degradation floor (Part 0.5). Any step that is intentionally out of tutorial order or skipped is flagged inline as intentional. HOSTED-AGENT PRECONDITION for Walk 7: a brand-new ADO org usually has ZERO Microsoft-hosted parallel jobs, so the operator must request the free grant (Organization Settings > Pipelines > Parallel jobs) days in advance, or configure a self-hosted pool - the spec names this precondition ('parallel-job grant or self-hosted agent sufficient to run one small pipeline'). What automation CANNOT check, and these walks exist for: a real, authenticated az PR create/complete against a live ADO host; a real Copilot-seat authentication and a real seat-driven verification round; and the first live execution of the azure-pipelines.yml (unit tests pin the command argv and the pipeline PARSES/structure-lints, but no CI run has ever executed it). The DELIBERATELY-NOT-WALKED case, same call as Set 078: a genuine seat auth-FAILURE walk would require deauthenticating a working staff seat, which is unsafe; the preflight's OK path (Walk 3) is the safe, repeatable check, and the tutorial's 'what failure looks like' Expect block documents the failure surface.

Review.Subarea: Walk 1 - install and authenticate the GitHub Copilot CLI seat
Review.ItemLabel: The standalone copilot CLI installs, logs in, and answers a real non-interactive prompt
Review.HumanAction:
Where you are: a fresh staff-like machine with VS Code, Python, and a valid Copilot license, at a terminal.
1. Install the standalone CLI:
   npm install -g @github/copilot
2. Confirm it resolves:
   copilot --version
3. Log in once (use the SECOND form only if your Copilot license lives on a GitHub Enterprise Cloud tenant):
   copilot login
   copilot login --host SUBDOMAIN.ghe.com
   Complete the device-flow prompt in the browser using the account that holds the Copilot license.
4. Prove a real authenticated call works:
   copilot -p "Write PI to 10 decimal places" --model claude-sonnet-4.6 --output-format json
Review.Expectation: Step 2 prints a version line, no error. Step 3's login completes without error (a credential is persisted to ~/.copilot/). Step 4 prints JSON (an assistant.message event with content) - NOT an auth prompt, NOT 'No authentication information found', NOT an error. This matches the tutorial's Part 0.6 exactly.
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: The seat's live authentication is genuinely operator-only (it needs a real license + a real browser device-flow login) - no automated path exists. The router's handling of this CLI's success and failure SHAPES is pinned by ai_router/tests/test_cli_transport.py and test_copilot_catalog.py against a fake transport; the live call here is what those fakes stand in for.

Review.Subarea: Walk 2 - create the ADO project/repo, grant membership, init and push the trunk
Review.ItemLabel: A brand-new scratch ADO project holds main with two files and the teammates can write to it
Review.HumanAction:
Where you are: still on the seat machine, Azure CLI signed in (az login) or AZURE_DEVOPS_EXT_PAT set to a Code Read & Write PAT.
1. In a NEW scratch Azure DevOps organization (never a real one), create a scratch PROJECT: New project > name 'hello-modules' > Visibility Private > Advanced > Version control = Git > Create. Then inside it, Repos > create an empty repository named 'hello-modules' (no README, no .gitignore) - or reuse the project's default repo.
2. In a terminal:
   mkdir hello-modules && cd hello-modules
   git init -b main
   echo "# hello-modules" > README.md
   echo ".venv/" > .gitignore
   git add README.md .gitignore
   git commit -m "chore: init trunk"
   git remote add origin https://dev.azure.com/{org}/{project}/_git/hello-modules
   git push -u origin main
3. In Project Settings > Permissions, add two test teammate accounts (or note they exist) to the project's Contributors group.
4. If using a PAT, prove it can read the project:
   az repos list --organization https://dev.azure.com/{org} --project {project} --output table
Review.Expectation: Step 2's push succeeds and the ADO repo's Files view shows main with exactly README.md and .gitignore. Step 3: the teammate accounts appear in the project's Contributors (they can clone/push/be requested as reviewers). Step 4 (PAT only): the command lists the repo in a table - a real authenticated read, not just 'the variable is set'. main is NOT yet policy-protected (that is Walk 4).
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: Creating a live ADO project/repo and granting project membership is entirely an operator-only, host-side action - there is no extension or router code path for it (spec non-goal: bootstrap stays a taught, one-time human setup). The remote-URL forms this produces are parsed by gitHost.test.ts.

Review.FunctionalArea: Build + Copilot seat profile (Part 2 + Part 0.6 preflight)
Review.Subarea: Walk 3 - Build project structure with the Copilot seat, then the auth-preflight
Review.ItemLabel: Build scaffolds the repo, writes transport.profile: copilot-cli, and the auth-preflight reports OK
Review.HumanAction:
Where you are: the hello-modules folder open in VS Code, containing only README.md and .gitignore.
1. Click the Dabbler AI Orchestration icon in the Activity Bar to open the Work Explorer (the Getting Started form shows).
2. In Section 1 (Build project structure): set Tier = Full; set Provider access = 'GitHub Copilot CLI seat'.
3. Click 'Build project structure'. Wait for it to finish (it checks the seat's model catalog at the end).
4. Open ai_router/router-config.yaml and find the transport block.
5. In a terminal at the repo root, run the auth-preflight:
   .venv\Scripts\python.exe -m ai_router.copilot_preflight   (Windows)
   .venv/bin/python -m ai_router.copilot_preflight            (macOS/Linux)
Review.Expectation: Step 3 creates .venv/, ai_router/, docs/session-sets/, the AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md/GEMINI.md instruction files, the two GitHub-flavored templates .github/CODEOWNERS and .github/workflows/monorepo-ci.yml (this ADO team does NOT use them - Walk 7 authors azure-pipelines.yml instead), and a Default module with 001-default-plan + 002-default-decomposition. The form is then replaced by the module tree. Step 4: router-config.yaml's transport block reads 'profile: copilot-cli' (the seat confirmed two distinct provider families; if it did not, the form/System Status strip said so honestly and Full tier is not usable on this seat - fall back to Lightweight). Step 5: the preflight prints an OK line (CLI present, credential present, a live probe authenticated). This matches the tutorial's Part 2 Expect block and Part 0.6 preflight note.
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: gitScaffoldSeatSetup.test.ts + copilotSeatSetup.test.ts pin the seat-setup probe -> config-write flow and the exact scaffolded file set on a fake spawner; the >=2-provider gate and transport.profile write are unit-covered. The OK verdict here is the live counterpart. The preflight's three-stage logic is pinned by ai_router/tests/test_copilot_preflight.py; the live authenticate is operator-only.

Review.Subarea: Walk 4 - define the three real modules and protect main
Review.ItemLabel: docs/modules.yaml declares three modules in order and a direct push to main is rejected
Review.HumanAction:
Where you are: the module tree after Build, Default still present.
1. Run the two starter sets once via Copilot Chat (paste each row's copied 'Start the next session of ...' line): 001-default-plan, then 002-default-decomposition. Then delete the Default module (hover its row > Delete Module...) and remove ALL practice output, including any NNN-default-* sets the decomposition session generated:
   rm -rf docs/session-sets/*-default-* docs/modules/default
2. In the returned Getting Started form (it only returns once the LAST default set is gone - the glob above removes the generated ones too), click 'Copy AI decomposition prompt', paste into Copilot Chat with the three-person team context, and save docs/modules.yaml so its modules: list is greeter, clock, integration (integration has touches: [greeter, clock]). Add an '# owners:' comment per module.
3. Commit and push: git add -A && git commit -m "chore: scaffold project structure and define modules" && git push
4. In ADO: Project Settings > Repositories > (repo) > Policies > Branch Policies on main. Turn on 'Require a minimum number of reviewers' and set the minimum to 1.
5. Deny push-bypass (easy to miss - the project creator/admin usually INHERITS it): Project Settings > Repositories > (repo) > Security. For the Contributors group AND your own account, set BOTH 'Bypass policies when pushing' and 'Bypass policies when completing pull requests' to an explicit Deny. Use Deny, NOT 'Not set': an admin usually inherits Allow from another group, and 'Not set' leaves that inherited Allow effective - only an explicit Deny overrides it.
6. Prove protection: on a throwaway edit, git commit and try git push origin main.
Review.Expectation: Step 2: the Work Explorer shows three module groups in manifest order - Greeter, Clock, Cross-Module Integration. Step 4: the reviewer-minimum policy shows as enabled on main. Step 5: 'Bypass policies when pushing' reads explicit Deny for your account and the Contributors group (not 'Not set' over an inherited Allow). Step 6: the direct push to main is REJECTED by ADO with a policy error (a PR is now required) - INCLUDING from the admin account. If your push still succeeds, your account's effective push-bypass is still Allow (it was left 'Not set' over an inherited Allow, not Denied) - set it to Deny and retry, or run the push from a plain Contributors account. This matches the tutorial's Part 3.
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: The module tree's grouping/order from docs/modules.yaml is pinned by the extension tree-provider suite (CustomSessionSetsView tests); ADO branch-policy configuration and the server-side push rejection are host-only actions with no local code path.

Review.FunctionalArea: Automated loop - Open PR on ADO (Part 4) [DISCHARGES SET 102 ARMED ADO UAT]
Review.Subarea: Walk 5 - author all three session sets (Parts 4-5) and open a real ADO PR via az
Review.ItemLabel: The three module session sets are authored and landed; the first uses a confirm-gated live ADO PR
Review.HumanAction:
Where you are: main window, modules declared, no session sets yet. You author ALL THREE session sets here (Part 4 for greeter, Part 5 for clock then integration) so the later walks have the state they need; the greeter PR is the live-ADO demonstration.
1. GREETER (Priya): ask Copilot Chat to write docs/modules/greeter/project-plan.md (scope: a greet() returning 'Hello, world!' + a unit test); save it. Command Palette > 'Dabbler: Generate Session-Set Prompt' > pick greeter; paste the copied prompt into Copilot Chat -> it writes docs/session-sets/001-greeter-hello/.
2. Land greeter as the live-ADO PR:
   git switch -c authoring/001-greeter-hello
   git add docs/modules/greeter/project-plan.md docs/session-sets/001-greeter-hello
   git commit -m "docs: greeter plan + session set 001-greeter-hello"
   Then Command Palette > 'Dabbler: Open PR for this set', accept the default PR title, read the modal, click 'Push + create PR'. Approve + Complete the PR in ADO, then: git switch main && git pull --ff-only && git branch -d authoring/001-greeter-hello.
3. CLOCK (Sam), after greeter merges: ask Copilot Chat to write docs/modules/clock/project-plan.md (scope: now_text() in services/clock/ returning HH:MM + a test); 'Dabbler: Generate Session-Set Prompt' > clock -> docs/session-sets/002-clock-hello/. Land it the same way on branch authoring/002-clock-hello; approve + complete; clean up.
4. INTEGRATION (Alex), after clock merges: git pull; ask Copilot Chat to write docs/modules/integration/project-plan.md (scope: compose greeter + clock into one program printing 'Hello, world! It is HH:MM.'); 'Dabbler: Generate Session-Set Prompt' > integration -> docs/session-sets/003-integration-compose/. Add prerequisites on 001-greeter-hello and 002-clock-hello (tell Copilot when pasting, or edit spec.md). Land it on branch authoring/003-integration-compose; approve + complete; clean up.
Review.Expectation: For the GREETER PR (step 2), the 'Dabbler: Open PR for this set' modal is titled 'Push this branch and open a PR?' and its detail lists, verbatim, in order: a 'git push -u origin authoring/001-greeter-hello' line and an 'az repos pr create --organization https://dev.azure.com/{org} --project {project} --repository {repo} --source-branch authoring/001-greeter-hello --target-branch main --title "..." --description ... --output json' line (org/project/repo derived from the remote - you never ran az devops configure), plus the line 'Target: azure-devops (dev.azure.com), base branch main.' The affirmative button reads 'Push + create PR'. After clicking: an information message reads 'PR created: https://dev.azure.com/{org}/{project}/_git/hello-modules/pullrequest/<number>' and that PR opens in the browser. THIS IS THE SET-102-ARMED live az PR create - record its outcome here and cross-reference in the disposition. After all three steps: docs/session-sets/ on main contains 001-greeter-hello, 002-clock-hello, 003-integration-compose (globally-unique, each stamped with its module:), and 003's row shows BLOCKED in the tree (Open Prerequisite Spec) until 001 and 002 complete.
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: gitWorkflow.test.ts 'Azure DevOps happy path' pins the exact az argv (derived org/project/repo) and the pullrequest URL parsed from the JSON id; gitHost.test.ts pins the ADO remote-URL parse table. The live authenticated create against a real ADO host is exactly what those fakes cannot exercise - this walk is that check.

Review.Subarea: Walk 6 - run the greeter and clock module sessions in worktrees, verified through the seat
Review.ItemLabel: Two worktree sessions implement their modules and complete real cross-provider verification via the Copilot seat
Review.HumanAction:
Where you are: all three sets authored and on main (Walk 5); integration is blocked on greeter+clock. Priya and Sam work in parallel.
1. GREETER (Priya): from the repo root, open the worktree:
   .venv\Scripts\python.exe -m ai_router.worktree open 001-greeter-hello   (Windows; .venv/bin/python on macOS/Linux)
   Open the sibling folder hello-modules-worktrees/001-greeter-hello/ in a NEW VS Code window; in its Work Explorer left-click the 001-greeter-hello row (the starter line copies); in that window's Copilot Chat (agent mode) paste the starter line and send it. Let the session run to the end (implement -> verify -> commit).
2. CLOCK (Sam), in parallel: same flow with 002-clock-hello (open its worktree, new window, paste the starter line, run to the end). Integration (003) stays blocked - do NOT run it yet (Walk 9).
Review.Expectation: Each session (greeter, then clock) creates hello-modules-worktrees/<slug>/ on branch session-set/<slug>. Copilot registers the session, writes the module's services/<mod>/ code + a test, and runs cross-provider verification THROUGH the Copilot seat - each reaches a real VERIFIED (or ISSUES_FOUND then fixed) verdict and commits to its session branch, with a session-events.jsonl ledger and a router-metrics row whose transport is 'copilot-cli'. A mis-authed seat would have BLOCKED the session at start (Set 086) rather than faking a verdict. The 003-integration row stays blocked (its prerequisites are not yet complete).
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: The worktree open/list mechanics are pinned by ai_router/tests/test_worktree*.py; the seat-transport verification path is pinned by test_copilot_routing_integration.py against a fake transport. A real seat-driven verification round is operator-only - this walk is the live proof that the whole Full-tier loop runs on a Copilot seat, not just provider API keys.

Review.FunctionalArea: Azure Pipelines CI + policies (Part 7) [FIRST-EVER LIVE PIPELINE RUN]
Review.Subarea: Walk 7 - author azure-pipelines.yml, register the pipeline, wire auto-reviewers + Build validation
Review.ItemLabel: The two-layer pipeline runs for real on a PR and on main; the required-check and auto-reviewer policies bind
Review.HumanAction:
Where you are: main checkout, sessions running. This is the first time azure-pipelines.yml is EXECUTED anywhere. ORDER MATTERS - ADO cannot register a pipeline from a YAML file it cannot see, so push first.
PRECONDITION: confirm hosted parallel capacity - Organization Settings > Pipelines > Parallel jobs. If hosted parallelism is 0 (common on a brand-new org), request the free grant AHEAD of the walk (it can take a couple of business days) or configure a self-hosted pool and change the pipeline's pool: block. The pipeline cannot run until this is in place.
1. Create azure-pipelines.yml at the repo root with the exact contents from the tutorial's Part 7.
2. Push it FIRST: git switch -c chore/guardrails && git add azure-pipelines.yml && git commit -m "build: activate Azure Pipelines monorepo CI" ; run 'Dabbler: Open PR for this set' and confirm ('Push + create PR'). Leave the PR open.
3. Register the pipeline: ADO > Pipelines > New pipeline > Azure Repos Git > hello-modules > Existing Azure Pipelines YAML file > Branch: chore/guardrails > /azure-pipelines.yml > Save (Save + run once).
4. Branch Policies on main > 'Automatically included reviewers' > + : add one Required entry per module with its path filter (greeter -> /services/greeter/*;/docs/modules/greeter/* ; clock -> /services/clock/*;/docs/modules/clock/* ; integration naming all three -> /services/integration/*;/docs/modules/integration/* ; a shared entry -> /docs/modules.yaml;/azure-pipelines.yml).
5. Branch Policies on main > 'Build validation' > + : pick the pipeline > mark Required.
6. Back on the chore/guardrails PR: queue the Build validation run (it was added after the PR opened), get approval, Complete (delete source branch), then git switch main && git pull --ff-only && git branch -d chore/guardrails.
Review.Expectation: Step 3 registers the pipeline with no YAML parse error (the file the tutorial ships parses and lints as valid Azure Pipelines) - and only succeeds because step 2 pushed the file first. The chore/guardrails PR run: the pipeline runs; per-module jobs (Test greeter/clock/integration module) SKIP because only azure-pipelines.yml changed, and the 'Detect changed modules' (changes) job succeeds (no git-fetch/credential error - the checkout uses persistCredentials and the explicit target refspec). On completion to main: the 'Test ALL modules (never vacuously green)' job runs and passes vacuously ('No services/ on main yet - guardrail passes vacuously.') exactly once, logging that line. Build validation shows as a Required check that must be green to complete; the auto-included-reviewers entries appear on subsequent PRs. This is the FIRST live validation of azure-pipelines.yml - record any deviation from the tutorial's Part 7 Expect here.
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: Session 1 machine-checked that the embedded azure-pipelines.yml PARSES and its job/condition structure is well-formed (yaml.safe_load + structural asserts), but NO run has ever executed it - live pipeline execution, Build-validation gating, and auto-included-reviewer binding are all ADO-server behaviors with no local code path. This walk is the sole executable validation.

Review.FunctionalArea: Automated loop - Finalize on ADO (Part 8) [DISCHARGES SET 102 ARMED ADO UAT]
Review.Subarea: Walk 8 - complete the greeter and clock session PRs on ADO and finalize both merges
Review.ItemLabel: After completing each session PR on ADO with delete-source-branch, one confirm-gated action syncs the trunk and cleans up
Review.HumanAction:
Where you are: the greeter and clock sessions (Walk 6) have committed code on session-set/001-greeter-hello and session-set/002-clock-hello. Do BOTH sets so Walk 9's 'greeter and clock merged' precondition holds.
1. GREETER: from its worktree window, run 'Dabbler: Open PR for this set' and confirm ('Push + create PR').
2. In the ADO PR page: Approve (as the reviewer), then Complete. In the completion dialog LEAVE 'Delete session-set/001-greeter-hello after merging' CHECKED (its default). Complete the merge.
3. In the MAIN checkout window (not the worktree), run git checkout main if needed, then 'Dabbler: Finalize merged set'; read the modal and click 'Finalize'.
4. CLOCK: repeat steps 1-3 for 002-clock-hello (Priya reviews Sam's PR).
5. Confirm: run 'git branch' and check the Work Explorer.
Review.Expectation: For each set, the 'Dabbler: Open PR for this set' modal title defaults to 'Session set <slug>'; after confirm the PR triggers only that module's path-scoped job (greeter's PR runs 'Test greeter module', not clock's, and vice-versa). Each 'Dabbler: Finalize merged set' modal is titled "Finalize merged set 'session-set/<slug>'?" and lists exactly, in order: 'git pull --ff-only', 'git worktree remove <path>', 'git branch -d session-set/<slug>', 'git fetch --prune'; it states deletion uses -d never -D; button 'Finalize'; an information message starting 'Merged set finalized.' lists each step's outcome. After both: the two worktrees are gone, main is fast-forwarded to both merges, 001-greeter-hello AND 002-clock-hello sit in the Complete bucket under their modules, 003-integration is now UNBLOCKED, and the 'Test ALL modules' job ran on each merge to main. THE GREETER PR + finalize round-trip IS THE SET-102-ARMED live ADO check - record its outcome and cross-reference in the disposition.
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: gitWorkflow.test.ts pins the worktree finalize path, idempotent re-run, and unmerged-branch refusal; the dogfood harness (gitWorkflow.dogfood.ts) runs push+finalize end-to-end against real git. The live ADO merge round-trip is exactly what those cannot cover.

Review.Subarea: Walk 9 - the integration PR auto-requests both touched owners
Review.ItemLabel: The integration set is run, reviewed by both touched owners, completed, and finalized to Complete
Review.HumanAction:
Where you are: greeter and clock merged to main; 003-integration-compose unblocked. This walk takes integration all the way to Complete so Walk 10's 'all three Complete' precondition holds.
1. As the integration owner (Alex): git pull ; then open the worktree:
   .venv\Scripts\python.exe -m ai_router.worktree open 003-integration-compose
2. Left-click the 003-integration-compose row, paste the starter line into Copilot Chat, and let the session compose services/integration/ (a test exercising the real greeter + clock code) and run to the end (implement -> verify -> commit).
3. From the worktree window, run 'Dabbler: Open PR for this set' and confirm ('Push + create PR').
4. Open the ADO PR page and look at the Reviewers panel.
5. Priya and Sam both Approve; wait for the 'Test integration module' Build validation to go green; then Alex Completes the PR (leave 'Delete session-set/003-integration-compose after merging' checked).
6. From the MAIN checkout window, run git checkout main if needed, then 'Dabbler: Finalize merged set' and click 'Finalize'.
7. Confirm the Work Explorer.
Review.Expectation: Step 4: because the auto-included-reviewers policy for /services/integration/* names all three, the PR auto-adds Priya and Sam as REQUIRED reviewers (Alex is the author, so his own vote does not count toward the minimum). The path-scoped 'Test integration module' job runs on the PR. Step 5: completion is blocked until BOTH required reviewers approve and Build validation is green - the touches discipline in practice, matching the tutorial's Part 9. Step 6's 'Dabbler: Finalize merged set' modal is titled "Finalize merged set 'session-set/003-integration-compose'?" and lists git pull --ff-only / git worktree remove <path> / git branch -d session-set/003-integration-compose / git fetch --prune; button 'Finalize'. Step 7: 003-integration-compose now sits in the Complete bucket under Cross-Module Integration, so ALL THREE sets are Complete; the 'Test ALL modules' job ran on the merge to main (the composed program's tests ran together for the first time).
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: Auto-included-reviewer binding on a path filter is an ADO-server behavior with no local code path; the touches->owners mapping intent is documented in docs/modules.yaml and audited by the companion review prompt. This walk is the live confirmation that the policy paths resolve to the right owners.

Review.Subarea: Walk 10 - cut a release tag, run the hotfix drill, run the rollback drill
Review.ItemLabel: The three confirm-gated pure-git release commands behave exactly as the tutorial documents
Review.HumanAction:
Where you are: all three sets Complete, main green, on an up-to-date main. This walks the FULL Part 10 hotfix flow, not just the tag commands.
1. TAG: run 'Dabbler: Cut release tag': tag name v0.1.0, Commit to tag = HEAD (default), message 'hello-modules 0.1.0'. Read the modal, click 'Create + push tag'.
2. DEPLOY: git checkout v0.1.0 ; python services/integration/app.py ; git switch main.
3. HOTFIX BRANCH: run 'Dabbler: Start hotfix from tag': pick v0.1.0, accept branch name hotfix/v0.1.0, click 'Create hotfix branch'. Make a small greeter fix, then git commit -am "fix(greeter): correct the greeting".
4. HOTFIX PR + CI: run 'Dabbler: Open PR for this set' and confirm; get a teammate approval and wait for the path-scoped 'Test greeter module' Build validation to go green (do NOT complete yet).
5. VALIDATE THE EXACT COMMIT: on the hotfix commit, run the full integrated suite locally (Bash): ( for d in services/*/; do python -m unittest discover -s "${d%/}" -v || exit 1; done ) && python services/integration/app.py
6. TAG THE FIX: still on hotfix/v0.1.0, run 'Dabbler: Cut release tag' for v0.1.1, Commit to tag = HEAD (the hotfix commit), message 'hello-modules 0.1.1 (hotfix)'.
7. DEPLOY + COMPLETE: git checkout v0.1.1 ; python services/integration/app.py ; git switch main. Then Complete the hotfix PR in ADO (delete source branch). Clean up: git switch main && git pull --ff-only && git branch -d hotfix/v0.1.0 && git fetch --prune.
8. ROLLBACK: run 'Dabbler: Roll back to tag': pick v0.1.0, click 'Check out tag'. Run python services/integration/app.py, then git switch main.
9. Confirm: git tag -l ; git for-each-ref refs/tags --format="%(refname:short) %(objecttype)".
Review.Expectation: Step 1's modal is titled "Cut and push release tag 'v0.1.0'?" and lists 'git tag -a v0.1.0 <sha> -m "hello-modules 0.1.0"' and 'git push origin v0.1.0' plus the resolved commit sha+subject; button 'Create + push tag'. Step 2: app.py prints the ORIGINAL greeting. Step 3's hotfix modal is titled "Start hotfix branch 'hotfix/v0.1.0' from 'v0.1.0'?" listing 'git switch -c hotfix/v0.1.0 v0.1.0'; button 'Create hotfix branch'. Step 4: the hotfix PR opens on ADO, the 'Test greeter module' job runs on it and goes green after approval. Step 5: every module's tests pass locally on the exact hotfix commit and app.py prints the FIXED greeting. Step 6: the v0.1.1 tag lands on the hotfix commit (v0.1.0 + only the fix), not on main. Step 7: v0.1.1 deploys with the fixed greeting, the PR completes, and the hotfix branch is deleted (the tag keeps the commit reachable). Step 8's modal is titled "Roll back to 'v0.1.0'?" listing 'git checkout v0.1.0', warns DETACHED HEAD; button 'Check out tag'; app.py then prints the PRE-fix greeting. Step 9: git tag -l lists v0.1.0 and v0.1.1, both objecttype 'tag' (annotated). Matches the tutorial's Part 10 end to end.
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: gitRelease.test.ts pins all three commands' argv, the mandatory confirm, sha-pinning, the existing-tag refusal, dirty-tree refusal, and annotated-tag creation; the S2 real-git dogfood ran the four drills against a scratch repo + bare remote. These are pure-git and host-agnostic, so the walk confirms the wording/flow on the live ADO clone rather than new host behavior.

Review.Subarea: Walk 11 (OPTIONAL) - Open PR with az hidden still pushes and opens the web create-PR page
Review.ItemLabel: Without a usable az CLI the command still pushes and opens the ADO create-PR web page with guidance
Review.HumanAction:
Where you are: the same live ADO clone; only if you want to see the degradation floor. This temporarily hides az from the extension.
1. In VS Code settings (Ctrl+,), search 'dabbler az' and set 'Dabbler Session Sets: Az Cli Path' to exactly: C:\does-not-exist\az.cmd
2. git switch -c session-set/uat-floor && echo floor > floor.txt && git add . && git commit -m "uat floor"
3. Run 'Dabbler: Open PR for this set', accept the default title, read the modal, click 'Push + create PR'.
4. Afterwards, clear the 'Az Cli Path' setting back to empty.
Review.Expectation: Step 3's modal lists the 'git push -u origin session-set/uat-floor' line and, instead of an az line, the line '(no az CLI found - the browser create-PR page will open instead)'. After clicking: the branch is pushed (pure git), the browser opens the ADO pullrequestcreate page with the source branch preselected, and a warning message includes install guidance ('winget install Microsoft.AzureCLI', 'az extension add --name azure-devops'). A broken explicit azCliPath is honestly reported, never silently bypassed to PATH. Matches the tutorial's Part 0.5 no-CLI floor note.
Review.ProgrammaticVerification: gitWorkflow.test.ts 'CLI absent' pins push-still-runs + the pullrequestcreate URL + the guidance text; hostCli.test.ts pins that an explicit-but-missing setting is never bypassed to PATH. This live spot check confirms the same behavior against a real ADO remote.
